entries.title: Stack A — Astro + Svelte Islands + Tailwind v4 + @vite-pwa/astro
entries.proposal: Adopt Astro 5 with Svelte 5 islands, Content Collections for MDX-backed lore, @vite-pwa/astro for PWA.
pros: Best runtime performance — islands ship ~0 JS by default, hydrate only interactive cards.
Content Collections + MDX make the lore browser nearly free.
Highest Lighthouse scores realistically achievable on GH Pages.
No framework lock-in on islands — add React/Solid per-page later.
cons: Two mental models — .astro files AND .svelte files, with island hydration semantics to learn.
Ill-suited to future SproutLab migration (SproutLab is app-shaped — islands fight that).
Higher cognitive load for a novice Builder.
entries.resolution: Rejected in favor of Stack B. Performance edge is real but invisible on 2026 mobile; learning-cost and cross-province transferability favored single-framework pick.

entries.title: Stack C — React SPA + Vite + TanStack Query + shadcn/ui + Tailwind + vite-plugin-pwa
entries.proposal: React 18+ Vite SPA, TanStack Query for Codex-JSON, shadcn/ui, vite-plugin-pwa.
pros: Largest ecosystem; shadcn/ui components polished and fully customizable.
TanStack Query ideal for live Codex reads.
Most abundant documentation and community support.
cons: Largest JS bundle of the three — worst mobile-install UX.
Biggest dependency tree; slowest installs in CI.
Same novice-friendliness issue as Stack A.
entries.resolution: Rejected. Stack B offers comparable capability with smaller bundle and simpler mental model.

entries.title: Stack D — Vite + Svelte 5 + Tailwind + vite-plugin-pwa (no SvelteKit)
entries.proposal: Plain Vite + Svelte + Tailwind, no SvelteKit. Smallest possible mental footprint.
pros: Absolute minimum cognitive load.
Fewest concepts to learn.
Works fine if Temple stays one page forever.
cons: No file-based routing.
No Content Collections — lore-as-markdown DIY.
Doesn't generalize to future SproutLab migration.
entries.resolution: Rejected. SvelteKit's routing and content-collection machinery pay back their small extra learning cost within a week.

entries.title: Use the Province Pattern (Single-File HTML PWA) for Temple
entries.proposal: Ship Temple as a single-file HTML PWA matching SproutLab / SEP Dashboard / SEP Invoicing / Command Center.
pros: Consistent with every other repo in the Order.
Zero build pipeline; install-anywhere-zero-config.
No dependency management burden.
cons: Temple's scope outgrows the single-file ceiling quickly.
Manifest + SW maintenance by hand is brittle at Temple's complexity.
No type-safety, no test harness, no component reuse.
Would hit Edict I (30K Rule) structurally rather than by growth.
entries.resolution: Rejected. This is exactly the case where Stack B pays for itself. Deliberate first departure from the province pattern.

entries.title: External Capture Mechanism — Google Sheets or Airtable
entries.proposal: Use a Google Sheet or Airtable base for session logs via form submissions; Temple reads via JSON export APIs.
pros: Lowest-friction data entry — web form, phone-friendly.
Zero code to write for the capture UI.
Built-in dashboards / filters.
cons: External dependency outside the Order's repo-based governance.
Session data lives off-Codex; violates 'Codex is source-of-truth' in spirit.
No versioning, no PR review trail.
Edict III only mechanically respected, not structurally.
entries.resolution: Rejected. Codex's snippet-import pipeline is canonical; extending with session_log type is zero-new-infrastructure.

entries.title: Direct Push to Main for One-Line Hotfixes
entries.context: Temple deploy fire-drill under time pressure
entries.proposal: Push the one-line deploy workflow fixes directly to main, skipping branch + PR.
pros: Saves ~30 seconds per fix in PR-opening overhead.
Fewer entries in the branches list.
cons: No atomic ratification record.
No test-plan checkbox trail for each fix.
Breaks the 'every change goes through PR' discipline.
Encourages the wrong default for future agent-driven work.
entries.resolution: Rejected. Held to branch + PR flow for all three deploy hotfixes.

entries.title: Classify Temple as Another Monument, Not a Watchtower
entries.proposal: Strip the Watchtower terminology. Call Temple a sibling Monument to Command Center.
pros: Doesn't introduce a new class into Book III.
Simpler for post-war Working Committee review.
Edict VI already covers Monuments; no amendment needed.
cons: Conflates observe and direct — distinct functional roles.
Command Center issues decrees; Temple cannot. Papers over a real asymmetry.
Future observer-class artifacts would benefit from Watchtower label.
entries.resolution: Unresolved. CHARTER proposes Watchtower; Working Committee to ratify post-war.

entries.title: Use System Font Stack for MVP to Defer Font Decision
entries.proposal: Ship v0.1 with ui-sans-serif, system-ui stack. Defer Playfair Display + Work Sans to v0.2.
pros: Saves a Google Fonts network fetch on first paint.
Zero font-related layout shift risk.
Font decision reconsiderable after dawn.
cons: Visual regression from Codex design system.
Temple looks generic where it should feel kin to Codex.
Saves trivial runtime cost.
entries.resolution: Rejected by Sovereign decree. Playfair + Work Sans locked for v0.1.

entries.title: Store Campaign State Inside Temple Repo
entries.proposal: Keep campaigns.json inside Temple rather than fetching from Codex. Updates via Temple PRs.
pros: Single-repo simplicity.
No cross-repo network dependency at runtime.
Faster page load (no live fetch).
cons: Violates Pillar II (Map ≠ Territory) — Temple becomes both map and territory.
Splits source-of-truth.
Every campaign update requires Temple PR + redeploy.
Schism with the snippet-import pipeline.
entries.resolution: Rejected. campaigns.json lives in Codex; Temple subscribes via raw.githubusercontent.com.
